speaker: interviewer
text: There was another episode?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. This I will always remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, this is tape three--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with Paul Goldstein.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The other episode that I'll always remember, I kind of started to call this in recent years, think about it as the day of the egg. The day of the egg. See there was little food around, and the local population got ration stamps. Now we did not have ration stamps, because although we were under their name, we didn't-- we were never officially registered on that name. We didn't have papers showing that we were really born as Roggemans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's OK to get by, but nothing to register as. So we didn't-- were no ration stamps for us. So the only food, so whatever food you-- you wanted to get beyond what the rations allowed was the food that Mr. Roggeman brought home from where he worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember him bringing in canteens and jars of-- of fairly healthy broths and food and meat and so forth, which as I said, we never touched, we never tasted. But then other food was all bought on the black markets. And the farmers were the ones that had the supplies. They had the meats and the-- and the-- and the butter and the white breads and the-- and the eggs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So eggs-- an egg was a very, very precious commodity. I remember on the black market an egg cost 25 francs. Now 25 francs in those days would be like a small fortune. I mean, I don't know what to compare it with because of the inflation. Money has changed value. Anyway, 25 francs was a lot of money. That same egg might-- might-- might have cost maybe one franc before the war. So when you went to-- to one of these farmers, you went and bought one egg. You didn't buy a dozen eggs. That was a purchase, a major purchase to buy one egg. So one day, Mrs. Roggeman sends me to this particular farm because we did all-- we did all the buying and shopping and everything else, to buy the egg. So I get to this farm, and they knew me already. And the farmer's son, all very healthy people and very well, a big boy. And I said, well-- he start to play with me. So I played with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and the way you played, a very popular sport in the fall or beginning of the winter was there are these wild chestnuts were growing on these trees and we used to knock them down because you'd play all kind of games with those chestnuts. So it was a question  of knocking down chestnuts from the tree. So I had so little opportunity to really play that it was so nice of the little kid to play with me. So the farmer boy played with me to throw down the chestnuts. And a very happy half hour. After the half hour, I wanted to take my hanky out  of my pocket and before I realized it that egg in my pocket had been crushed in my pocket from playing with this boy. And I start to get panicky, because I knew what was going to happen to me if I come home without the egg or with a-- and with a dirty pocket and without the 25 francs.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I started to cry. And I told the boy, I says look, I says, I really can't go home without the egg. Is there any way that you could give me another egg? There was no way. It was like asking for diamonds for free. [SIGHS] So I was afraid to go home. So I said, would you please go home-- it was a bigger, much bigger boy than I was-- would you please come with me because my mother will be very, very upset and she won't-- will you come and tell what happened, that you played with me and that I didn't do anything wrong, that it just was an accident? So because I'm afraid to go home by myself. So this he agreed to. So I went home. I rang the doorbell. And she opened the door. What is it? Mrs. Roggeman, Paul came to buy the egg, and we played and while playing accidentally the egg got broke in his pocket. The egg is broken. And it's not really his fault, blah, blah, blah. She says, fine, thank you. And the boy left, and she closed the door. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The savage beating I got for that egg, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, the relative value of-- of-- of an egg or a piece of bread, you know, now you go and buy eggs by the dozen, but that was the day of the egg. The worst beating I ever had in my life. Took me quite a while to recover from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she beat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, just blindly, body, head. I-- I learned also to-- to-- to protect myself by going into a crouch or to protect my face and my head. It was very savage--

speaker: interviewer
text: With fists?

speaker: interviewee
text: --the punishments. And no food. She always used to grab something. I forgot really, I can't-- I forgot the detail of that. But-- but-- and then all kinds of punishment, like stay in your room and don't come out and don't this. Whatever little food we had, three days no food, three days no leaving the room, this kind of stuff. So it was the day of the egg. And that was her. So beatings would be almost like daily. Though-- we did very hard work. We cut the wood in the wood shed. We had to-- we looked after the vegetable garden. We had to carry this basket with all the clothes to Boechout, which was another village. We went to church in Edegem. We lived in Hove. And went to school for a while in [INAUDIBLE]. And we went to the laundry in Boechout. And all these are two, three kilometers. So my brother and I used to drag this heavy basket with laundry. Had to stop every-- we were very weak, so every 10, 15 minutes, we had to stop because we just couldn't carry it. On the way, naturally, there was the odd cherry tree, apple tree, there was a season. So we used to stop and-- and-- and feed ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was that kind of work. Practically no food, no drink, and be beating almost every day. That was our life. And what if-- if you asked why or if you protested?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was out  of the question. It was-- for-- for not doing anything wrong or inadvertently making-- slipping up once in a while we got treated like that. Any-- any-- any question would've been an act of-- I don't recall even, it wasn't even an option to-- to-- to ask anything. It was ironic that I would have to say that during World War II, between the people-- between the first two years and the years in hiding, you know, we circulated as their children. And-- and we talked to neighbors and we met all kinds of people, the local policeman, and the-- and the shepherd, and-- and the people in all the stores that we want to shop. And some people were collaborators with the Germans and some were not. We never knew who was who, so we had to be very, very careful not to give ourselves away. And so most of the-- so it was ironic. I would say that she, Mrs. Roggeman, was probably the worst person in terms of-- of evil that I encountered during that war. Because I physically experienced her physical abuse. But almost unbelievably, despite of everything that happened by the Germans, I would have to say that the nicest person that I met in World War II happened to be a German officer. A German officer was the nicest person I met in World War II. It so happened that there was a German major of the German Transportation Division of the Wehrmacht who had been a friend of the Roggemans before they took us in hiding. And the way he became a friend was that the Germans occupations, they-- whenever there was people had a free room or didn't have kids, they would billet their officers in private homes. And he was billeted with the Roggemans prior to us being taken in by them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he knew that they had no children. And he was a friend of the house, and he continued to visit even after. As a matter of fact, he used to come over on the train and then jump off just in front of the house where we were. So he knew that we were Jewish. And he-- it was his duty to turn us in, and turn them in as well. And yet, he-- he didn't do any of the sort. Not only that, he went out of his way to be nice to us and kind to us. He played with us on his knee and he-- and he-- and-- we could talk freely to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he also brought-- every time he came he brought presents. And he brought us, I remember, these poppy seeds cakes which was a German delicacy. And the sour-- sour bread that they used to make. And-- and for us, it was all, it was never meal time. So we were supposed to only eat at meal time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we never had one crumb of everything he brought. The  thought was-- we never-- Mrs. Roggeman would take it away, and they would eat it themselves. We never got, but you're not supposed to tell him. Because if we were ever to tell him I don't know what she would have done to us. So he thought he was being nice. He was being nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, there were some episodes with him that are just as unforgettable in-- in the positive sense as the other ones, horrible ones that were unforgettable. It really helped you strike a balance, even those atrocious times. My experience with this officer who I've tried to locate since. But I haven't been able to. I never knew-- I don't remember his name. I don't know if I ever knew his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you called him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember  what they called. I remember what he looked like. I would even recognize him, because I have a very good photographic memory. I think that deliberately they wouldn't tell his name so that I would not think if I was ever caught I would not blurt out his name. I think it was deliberate. But I can't prove that, but  that's just an afterthought. Because I would remember his name if-- if his name was given. The-- but a couple of episodes with him are worth talking about to put things in perspective. That even in the worst of times, where-- your worst of enemies, there's always going-- an exception. There's always some kind people. So I've learned-- I learned-- learned through that to never be prejudice, never bigoted, and only to judge people on their own merit, no matter what their origin, no matter where they belong. It completely influenced my life in terms of attitude towards other people. As a matter of fact, as far as I'm concerned, from then on I've classified people in two categories, two categories, good people and bad people. Because a person's identity is a matter that a person has no choice over, his origin. Nobody chooses to be born Jewish or Christian, black or white, an Eskimo or an Aborigine in the Amazon, but everybody is in control as to how he or she behaves towards her fellow man. And to me, that's the only yardstick that I judge people by. There's only two divisions, two kinds of people. And in the kind of episode, incident that brought this, that infuses in me and germinated in me and developed into my adult life. Well, first of all, take 1943. Incidentally, the Roggemans did have a radio, despite of the fact it was not allowed. It was hidden in some kind of cupboard and was always put on very quietly. But you know, like a blind person will-- will-- will develop his sense of hearing very acutely, so we-- I developed a sense of hearing because we were not supposed to hear or listen and to know. So I heard everything. And so I heard the news, the BBC news. Matter of fact, it came-- it came through and there was always these scrambling sounds. You know, for instance, I remember in-- in-- in-- in German and in French and Dutch how the BBC used to say, here's the BBC. This is the radio of free Belgium, or free France. And then-- and then the scramble, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom. And in between there would be the news. From that, I used to know everything. As-- like today, like today, I'm aware of what's going on in the world. I used to know. From-- the Roggemans never knew that I heard or listened. I never gave away that I heard or listened. But somehow I had such a keen sense of hearing developed because of that, that I knew exactly what was going on. And the point that this is leading to is that 19-- 1943, in the fall when this all started, the Germans were at the height of their military power. That's when they were on their march. The east front had been opened. And they were moving towards Moscow and Stalingrad and Leningrad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every day on the news there was another Polish or Russian town being taken. So all you heard on the news, today the-- the Germans took Smolensk and Bialystok. These names still ring in the back of my mind from those days because I remember those words. So when-- when he came in one day to visit he was still a German officer. He was still proud to belong to this conquering nation. And he-- he came in proud and saying, another victory. Today we took this. Today with took that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hear him. I'm this little snot nose, this little Jewish kid they are hidden from him. And I look at him, and I don't remember, I can't tell you what made me say what I said, but I did literally tell him-- look, I told him. I says, today you may do-- be very victorious, something to that effect. I says, but don't worry, I told him, our day will come too. I really told him that in '43. [LAUGHS] In this circumstance, I didn't realize what I was doing. If perhaps being a few years older I would never have said that. But I said that and he did not take offense. Not only not take offense, he comes in a few days later. And-- and this is really the-- the focus of what I'm trying to say, bring to, because it's, I think, such an important episode. Illustrate this-- this-- this total almost impossible contradiction in terms of who was nice and who was good. That-- he comes in with a camera. And he says to Mrs. Roggeman, I'd like to take your family picture. Let's go in the garden. Let's take all of you. I'd like to have a souvenir.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then this-- some of these episodes are so engraved in your memory that you'll never forget. And he says, [INAUDIBLE] here's was happened yesterday. And he turns around and says to her, she says to him, sorry. She looks at us and says to him, what about them? Just like that, you know. What about them? Want them in the picture too? So he turns to her and he says the following in German. He says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Unbelievable. In English, and why not? After all, they're also human beings. This-- this-- this little dialogue has been so part of my heart and soul and mind for so many years that the-- than an officer, a major of the army of the occupying forces that we were supposed to be protected from should have to teach the so-called good Christians who hid us from him about humanity. How can you possibly be-- be totally against any people 100%? How can you not allow for the exception? Why not treat people on their own merit? He instilled in me a sense of non bias and non-bigotry which was lasted me forever and will last me forever. So that to me was an unbelievable moment which I-- such an impact. Here this hell, this private concentration camp of the Roggemans, and then here this wonderful, warm person telling her that we're also human beings. What can I say? These are some-- these are highlights and lowlights if there is such an expression. You told me at one point you went with Mrs. Roggeman to see your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes, yes, yes. That's another-- another very special episode. What happened was that after we had been with the Roggemans for three months, and she was supposed to-- to go at least once a month to my mother in hiding to collect the month's rent and the money. My mother didn't want to give her all the money up front. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So wonderful news one day. We're going to visit my mother. My mother was in a-- in a house in the suburb of [INAUDIBLE], which is a suburb of Brussels with a very, very nice elderly couple. I think their name were the [? Kersters, ?] I believe was there name, something like that. And we were supposed to visit her. My mother wanted to see us. And we would go and journey with Mrs. Roggeman to Brussels by train to visit my mother. So here it's a wonderful, what wonderful news, because we were very lonesome for-- for-- for her. And so here we go. And we arrive. And the door opens, and I'm ready to embrace my mother. And here instead of my mother stands my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Absolutely, total shock. How is this possible? How could my father be there? He was supposed to be in the labor camp. And your mother was not there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was not there. So my father told me, us, brother and I, that he had received a pass for-- for a few days of furlough, and that my mother was simply on some errands. I do remember that I realized even then, how  I realized, I don't know, but I did realize that it was impossible for anybody to have a pass from the German-- from the German labor camp. But I didn't want to worry him. So I acted as if I accept his explanation. They shouldn't worry that I should talk or speak outside. So he believed that I believed that he was on furlough. Matter of fact, something very interesting happened, which showed me the kind of person my father was. That in spite of not being observant, how strong he-- how strong he was in terms of his identity. So he started asking all kinds of questions. How things were going and-- and-- and this. Now you must remember, this is now end of '43 in the height of the occupation. And at that moment, I did not know what happened, how come he-- why he was there and my mother wasn't there. I found out later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before I get to what happened, I just want to recall what happened from my perspective. So here my father asked how things are. On the one hand, I got to be very careful. And she hadn't reached the-- the height of her-- of intensity yet in her treatment of us. This just grew worse and worse, still at the very beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but we were going to church and saying the prayers. And all this came out from our little story. So my father was very, very mad. That's the kind of person. So he went and brought out a [INAUDIBLE], and he said [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to me, read Hebrew. Read. Didn't know what to do. I was not supposed to by her and was afraid of what she was going to do when we get home because I was supposed to be a Christian. And here my father wants me to read. So I hesitate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father gave me a backhand right then and there. Slap in the face. My head almost turned. And he says, read! Just I don't want you to forget that you are a Jew. [CRYING] And I read. That was the visit. We never had another visit. I don't know if it was because of that or was because it became more dangerous. That is the last-- only-- only visit in September. I found out later what-- what-- what this switch came from. Why-- why we went to visit my mother. It so happens that it was the custom for the organization told with the slave labor that after three months, I understood-- I've been told afterwards was told after, that after three months of hard labor the people automatically were shipped off to Auschwitz and then new people be brought in after them. So  they had an unlimited supply of people. And it was just so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father was sent on a transport to Auschwitz three months after he was taken away. So that means we're talking here he was-- he was taken away probably I would say August. Went into hiding in September. So October, November he was on this-- on this transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans again said we're going to relocate you. You're going to go to other labor camps. That was just-- just to be more efficient to prevent kind of revolts and all that stuff they would tell the people it was always the good news. But always there were-- there were rumors. Always rumors percolating. And my father was the kind of person that believed the rumors. He believed the worst. He didn't want to-- to put his head-- bury his head  in the sand and believe that everything was going to be OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the train with all these people to Auschwitz passed through the southern tip of Belgium called the Ardennes, the mountains, and they were-- and they were looking through holes in the-- in-- in the walls in-- In the boxcar?

speaker: interviewee
text: --the boxcar, he-- he more or less knew that they were still in Belgium. And apparently he told himself-- by the way, his brother was on the same transport, but not in the same car. My Uncle Jacques. Because they were taken together in the same transport. And he looked around and there was another chap with him by the name of Joseph [? Poznanski ?] who had met in the camp, and they became friends. And my father said to Joseph [? Poznanski, ?] says, if we-- If we go we'll never see our families again. That man's family was also hidden. But they were not hidden by Christians. They were hidden-- a mother with her children together in Antwerp in a place. And again, non-observant Flemish-speaking family. Very ironic, something not too pleasant to dwell on, but there's some kind of a message there. Anyway, let's get back to what happened. So my father said, we have to get out this train before we get into Germany. I know there were machine guns on the roofs and there were search lights. If you visit the Holocaust Memorial Museum in Washington, you saw that car-- that car. As a matter of fact, when I went there last year for the first time saw the kind of car that my dad was on when he was on this transport to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father felt it was better to try an escape and make the best of it even if he got shot there then to keep going. That was his attitude. So-- so him and his friend Joseph, also [? went ?] Joseph [? Poznanski. ?] Actually jumped and rolled into the ditch, apparently other people jumped too. Many were shot. But they managed to hide in the ditch and the train went on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his friend Joseph, as matter, twisted his ankle. And my father didn't abandon him there. They turned their coats inside out so that the Star of David wouldn't show. And then by night, they traveled on foot. And my father somehow, again, through some like letter smuggling I think, knew the address where my mother was hidden and he found it. And he appeared in my mother's place after stalking the place for a few days and waiting for a safe moment. I think my mother used to have her sister that I mentioned, also hidden nearby, Rosa, who didn't have a husband. I think they had divorced, her husband had left. And my mother used to take-- my mother had the money in the family. The others were poor. Used  to take supplies and money at least once a week. So my father, in the night, stalked the place till he saw my mother, and then he we reunited with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now my father and mother are together. Wonderful. Hidden in that same place. And all this happened between the time that we were-- we had planned to go  and visit my mother. So if nothing had changed we would have seen both my father and my mother. Unfortunately, a week later, my mother, on one of her little trips to the building where my aunt was hidden, happened to walk in there exactly when there was a raid on the house. Somebody [? else ?] [? told. ?] And all the Jews in that building were arrested and my mother as well. My mother was taken to a camp. So my father escaped from a camp, finds my mother, and a week later my mother gets caught. And this is how I turned out, it turned out to see my father and not my mother. Where was your mother sent when she was arrested?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, any Jew arrested in Belgium caught during the war was sent to a camp in the city of Mechelen, Malines, which is about halfway between Brussels and Antwerp. And there was a German military headquarters for the area that was called Kazerne Dossin officially. Like you have Drancy or whatever in Paris and you have whatever the name is in Holland. Kazerne Dossin was the assembly camp in Belgium where all the Jews that were arrested or caught were assembled. An then every 15th of the month, a train full of people used to be sent to Auschwitz from that-- from that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother was caught in that camp around October of 1942. 28,000 people went to the camp, Belgian Jews. A few hundred were alive at the-- when the camp was liberated. My mother was one of those few hundred. Absolutely unbelievable. She endured quite a bit of hardship, but she wasn't in Auschwitz. She was in that camp. She came out at 75% invalid for doing all kinds of things, was beaten and all that. But how did she survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a manicurist in the beauty salon. And the first commandant needed a manicurist, wanted a volunteer. My mother saw-- by the way, my mother's sister was caught with two children, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Paula and Maurice, little children, beautiful little girl, Paula. And-- and because of that, they went right away on transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother-- my mother was right in separating, which was a tough thing to do for mothers. But she was-- she-- but also, she could afford it and my aunt couldn't. So she saw-- she saw my aunt with the children go on this transport. So she was a manicurist of the commandant, so she stayed a few months like that. We're going to take a short break.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
